content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

March 19, 1997

Mr. Robert E. Leidigh
Law Offices of Olson, Hagel, Fong
Leidigh, Waters & Fishburn, LLP
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-97-140

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

     This letter is a response to your request for advice on behalf of Assembly Speaker Cruz
Bustamante regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

     May a member of the state Legislature sign a solicitation letter for a fundraising event for
a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization if the member serves on the board of directors of the
association that determines where or how those funds are eventually used and the member lends
his or her name to the event?

CONCLUSION

     Yes. Proposition 208 did not change existing law with regard to candidates soliciting
funds for nonprofit organizations. Under the facts described, the organization is not candidate
controlled and, therefore, no contributions to the legislator's campaign committee will result. In
addition, no contribution to officeholder accounts will result, nor gifts to the attending
legislators.

FACTS

     The Bob Moretti Memorial Scholarship Foundation will be holding their annual
fundraiser, which is a golf tournament, in April 1997. The purpose for the annual event is to

_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18000 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-97-140
Page 2

raise funds to provide an academic scholarship for post-secondary education to a qualified
California resident and to provide funds to endow a perpetual scholarship fund in the name of
Robert Moretti at the University of Notre Dame. The event will be entitled the Speaker-
President Pro Tem Charity Golf Tournament. The golf tournament will feature two divisions
contested, the Speaker's Shootout and the Pro Tem Scramble.

The Bob Moretti Scholarship Foundation is a nonprofit organization established as a
charitable corporation under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. It has a board of 15
members, only four of whom are "candidates" or agents, etc. of candidates. Eleven, or two-
thirds of the members, are not candidates, their agents, or relatives of the candidates. The
Speaker of the Assembly and the President pro Tempore of the Senate have served as co-chairs
of the foundation in the past. They are not involved in the day-to-day functioning of the
foundation. However, they have traditionally chaired the annual meeting at which scholarship
recipients are determined.

It has been traditional for the Speaker of the Assembly and the President pro Tem to sign
solicitation letters in connection with the event and to lend their names to the annual event.
It is contemplated that solicitation letters signed by the Speaker of the Assembly and the
President pro Tempore seeking contributions to the Bob Moretti Memorial Scholarship
Foundation will be sent to a wide list of potential donors, which includes entities that are lobbyist
employers, lobbying firms, and lobbyists. Legislators are normally invited to attend the golf
tournament and/or the awards banquet that follows, and their attendance is without charge to
them.

ANALYSIS

Contributions

Prior to passage of Proposition 208 on November 5, 1996, payments received or behested
by an elected official were considered campaign contributions, unless some exception applied.
Section 82015 defines "contribution" as any payment, forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan
by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full and
adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is
not made for political purposes. A payment is made for political purposes if it is: (1) for the
purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualified or passage of any
measure; or (2) received by or made at the behest of a candidate. (Regulation 18215(a).)²

Section 82007 defines "candidate," in pertinent part, as any individual who is listed on

2    A candidate "behests" a contribution when the contribution is made "under the control or at the direction
of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the express,
prior consent of." (Regulation 18225.7(a).)

File No. A-97-140
Page 3

the ballot for elective office. An individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or her
status as a candidate until such time as that status is terminated pursuant to section 84214. Thus,
legislators are considered candidates under section 82007 and any payment they bhest are
presumed to be for political purposes and could be considered contributions. (Danner Advice
Letter, No. A-96-056.)

The contribution limitations contained in section 85301 set limits on contributions made
"to any candidate or the candidate's controlled committee." (Section 85301, emphasis added.)
Section 85305 contains time restrictions on when a candidate may accept contributions.
Contributions a candidate solicits for himself or herself, or for the candidate's controlled
committee, will be subject to the limits set forth in sections 85301 and 85305.³ Conversely, if
the candidate solicits contributions for a committee or organization not controlled by the
candidate, then the limits in sections 85301 and 85305 do not apply.

Regulation 18215(c)(4) provides that communications are not contributions if they do not
contain express advocacy, do not make references to the candidate's candidacy for elective
officer or the candidate's opponent for elective office, and do not solicit contributions to the
candidate or to third persons for use in support or in opposition to the candidate's opponent.
We have also advised that when a sponsor, such as a nonprofit entity, handles the specific
arrangements concerning an event (e.g., the acquisition of food) and this is not performed in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with
the express prior consent of the official, then the payments would not be considered contributions
to the official. (Schmidt Advice Letter, No. A-96-096; Schmidt Advice Letter, No. A-96-171;
and Wierbinski Advice Letter, No. A-96-106.)

Proposition 208 did not amend the definition of a controlled committee. A "controlled
committee" is defined in the Act as:

"[A] committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by a
candidate or state measure proponent or which acts jointly with a
candidate, controlled committee or state measure proponent in
connection with the making of expenditures. A candidate or state
measure proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or any
other committee he controls has a significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the committee." (Section 82016.)

The Commission has interpreted the definition of "controlled committee" broadly to
include any significant participation in the actions of a committee by a candidate, his or her

³ In addition, please keep in mind that no elected officeholder may solicit or accept campaign
contributions "from, through, or arranged by a registered state or local lobbyist if that lobbyist finances, engages, or
is authorized to engage in lobbying the governmental agency for which the candidate is seeking election or the
governmental agency of the officeholder." (Section 85704.)

File No. A-97-140
Page 4

agent, or representatives of any other committee he or she controls. (Higdon Advice Letter, No.
I-94-189.) Therefore, if a candidate solicits funds for a committee, but does not control the
committee as described above, then the limitations in sections 85301 and 85305 will not apply.
Similarly, if a candidate solicits funds for a nonprofit organization, the limitations in sections
85301 and 85305 will not apply unless the organization is actually the candidate's controlled
committee.

Section 82016 basically describes two ways in which an organization may become a
candidate's controlled committee. First, the candidate might exert significant influence on the
actions or decisions of an organization. Therefore, we have advised that where a candidate is a
voting member of an organization's leadership, the candidate is presumed to be the controlling
candidate since the candidate exercises significant influence on the actions or decisions of the
organization. (Ferguson Advice Letter, No. A-86-044; Gastelum Advice Letter, No. A-96-113.)
Where a candidate was the president of the board of directors of a nonprofit organization
established to raise scholarship funds, we concluded he had significant control over the
scholarship funds, and therefore, the fund was considered his controlled committee. (Terrell
Advice Letter, No. I-90-730.)

Another way an organization may become a controlled committee is where candidates act
in concert with the organization in making campaign expenditures. By coordinating the
expenditures, a candidate, in effect, can bolster his or her campaign for office through the
organization. (Section 82025; Regulation 18225; Casey Advice Letter, No. A-93-082; Gastelum
Advice Letter, supra.)

However, regulation 18217, adopted in 1990, provides an alternative definition of
controlled committee applicable to organizations that are tax exempt under section 501 of the
Internal Revenue Code. Subdivision (a) of regulation 18217 provides that a nonprofit
association will be considered a controlled committee if both of the following apply:

"(1) A candidate, his or her agent, or any committee he or she
controls, exercises significant influence over the actions and
decisions of the organization, or acts jointly with the organization
in connection with the making of expenditures.
(2) The organization qualifies as a committee under Government
Code Section 82013(a), and the organization is operated for
political purposes. For purposes of this regulation, an organization
is 'operated for political purposes' if either of the following applies:
(A) The organization receives or expends funds for the purpose of
influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for
or against the nomination or election of a candidate or the
qualification or passage of any measure.
(B) The organization makes contributions to candidates or their
controlled committees." (Emphasis added.)

File No. A-97-140
Page 5

Pursuant to regulation 18217(c), we presume that most tax-exempt organizations are *not*
operated for political purposes if:

"(1) The organization does not make contributions to candidates.
(2) The name of the organization does not include the name of the
candidate. For purposes of this subdivision (c)(2) the term 'name
of the candidate' means the candidate's first and last name or some
other unambiguous reference to the candidate.
(3) The organization does not spend funds in excess of the amount
permitted under Section 501(h) of the Internal Revenue Code to
influence or attempt to influence legislative action.
(4) The organization does not spend funds to influence or attempt
to influence the qualification or passage of any measure in an
amount sufficient to qualify the organization as a committee under
Section 82013 of the Government Code."

The purpose of subdivision (a)(2) of regulation 18217 is to avoid classifying a
nonpolitical nonprofit organization as a controlled committee merely because donations are
received at the behest of candidates. (*Evans Advice Letter*, No. I-91-031; *Mello Advice Letter*,
No. A-91-150; *Casey Advice Letter*, No. A-93-082 .) Therefore, the mere fact that an elected
officer solicits donations for a nonprofit organization does not mean that those donations are
"contributions." A person who makes a donation to a nonprofit organization which is tax-
exempt under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code is not making a political
contribution unless the persons knows or has reason to know that the organization is operated for
political purposes. (Regulation 18217(e); *Statham* 1-90-246; *Gastelum Advice Letter*, *supra*.)

According to the facts that you have provided, it does not appear that the nonprofit
organization described in your letter would be operated for political purposes. Presumably, the
organization will not make contributions to candidates or spend funds to influence legislative
action or to support or oppose ballot measures.⁴ The event in question will include the title of
the members, but the organization does not include their names. Therefore, even if a candidate
exercises control over a nonprofit organization, it will not be considered a controlled committee
unless facts exist indicating that it is operated for political purposes. (Regulation 18217(f).)

We note that regulation 18217 also provides for a presumption that a nonprofit
organization is not a controlled committee for purposes of subdivision (a)(1) of the regulation if

___________________________

⁴ However, please note, if the organization were to make contributions to the members, it would run the
risk of being treated as a controlled committee. (*Gastelum Advice Letter*, *supra*.) Regulation 18217 essentially
provides that donations to a nonprofit are not considered contributions to a candidate because the organization
would not otherwise make contributions to candidates.

File No. A-97-140
Page 6

certain criteria are met. Subdivision (b) of regulation 18217 provides that a nonprofit is
presumed not to be significantly influenced by a candidate if:

"(1) The candidate is not substantially involved in the day-to-day
operations of the organization, and the organization is controlled
by a board of directors with 3 or more members, two-thirds of
whom are not:
(A) Candidates;
(B) Agents, campaign staff, employees, or persons otherwise
under the control of a candidate; or
(C) Brothers, sisters, parents, children, spouses, brothers-in-law,
sisters-in-law, sons-in-law, daughters-in-law, mothers-in-law or
fathers-in-law of a board member who is a candidate.
(2) The name of the organization does not include the name of the
candidate. For purposes of this subdivision (b)(2) the term 'name
of the candidate' means the candidate's first and last name or some
other unambiguous reference to the candidate."

Under your facts, over two-thirds of the members are not candidates or their agents. As
noted above, the organization does not include their names. While the Speaker of the Assembly
and the President pro Tempore are on the board of directors and will participate in deciding who
are the scholarship recipients, they do not participate in the day-to-day activities of the nonprofit.
Therefore, the presumption of regulation 18217 is met.

Officeholder Accounts

When the voters enacted Proposition 208, the initiative established the option of
noncampaign officeholder accounts. Section 85313 provides that an officeholder may accept
contributions "for expenses related to assisting, serving, or communicating with constituents, or
with carrying out the official duties of the elected officer, provided aggregate contributions to
such funds do not exceed ten thousand dollars ($10,000) within any calendar year and that the
expenditures are not made in connection with any campaign for elective office or ballot
measure." The Commission plans to adopt a regulatory amendment that will more specifically
regulate contributions made to officeholder accounts and payments to support community,
legislative, and governmental public events cosponsored by officials. (Gallegos Advice Letter,
No. A-97-052, Leideigh Advice Letter, No. A-97-126.)

According to your facts, section 85313 does not apply. The fundraising event does not
appear to be related to assisting, serving, or communicating with constituents, nor is it related to
carrying out the official duties of the elected officers. Moreover, you have not indicated that any
monetary contributions will be deposited into any officeholder accounts. Therefore, no
contributions to the officeholder account will result.

File No. A-97-140
Page 7

Gifts

Payments may be considered gifts under some circumstances, subject to the $290 gift
limit and the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Sections 82028, 87100-87103, and
89503.) Generally, when persons donate money to a nonprofit at a public official's behest, the
donations will not be treated as gifts. (In re Nejedly, 2 FPPC Ops. 45; Stratton Advice Letter,
No. I-96-213.)

You state in your letter that the legislators will attend the fundraising event at no charge
to them. Under Commission regulation 18946.4, a ticket for a fundraising event of an
organization established under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code has no value.
Therefore, attendance at the charitable fundraising event at no charge will not result in a gift to
the legislators.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
Senior Staff Counsel, Legal Division

association that determines where or how those funds are eventually used and the member lends 
his-or her name to the event?

addition, no contribution to officeholder accounts will result, nor gifts to the attending 
legislators.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Maya member of the state Legislature sign a solicitation letter for a fundraising event for 
a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization if the member serves on the board of directors ofthe 
association that determines where or how those funds are eventually used and the member lends 
his-or her name to the event?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Proposition 208 did not change existing law with regard to candidates soliciting 
funds for nonprofit organizations. Under the facts described, the organization is not candidate 
controlled and, therefore, no contributions to the legislator'~ campaign committee will result. In 
addition, no contribution to officeholder accounts will result, nor gifts to the attending

embedding.first_500_words: RAVI MEHTA CHAIRMAN FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION March 19, 1997 Mr. Robert E. Leidigh Law Offices of Olson, Hagel, Fong Leidigh, Waters & Fishburn, LLP 555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425 Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-97-140 Dear Mr. Leidigh: This letter is a response to your request for advice on behalf of Assembly Speaker Cruz Bustamante regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTION May a member of the state Legislature sign a solicitation letter for a fundraising event for a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization if the member serves on the board of directors of the association that determines where or how those funds are eventually used and the member lends his or her name to the event? CONCLUSION Yes. Proposition 208 did not change existing law with regard to candidates soliciting funds for nonprofit organizations. Under the facts described, the organization is not candidate controlled and, therefore, no contributions to the legislator's campaign committee will result. In addition, no contribution to officeholder accounts will result, nor gifts to the attending legislators. FACTS The Bob Moretti Memorial Scholarship Foundation will be holding their annual fundraiser, which is a golf tournament, in April 1997. The purpose for the annual event is to _________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18000 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-97-140 Page 2 raise funds to provide an academic scholarship for post-secondary education to a qualified California resident and to provide funds to endow a perpetual scholarship fund in the name of Robert Moretti at the University of Notre Dame. The event will be entitled the Speaker- President Pro Tem Charity Golf Tournament. The golf tournament will feature two divisions contested, the Speaker's Shootout and the Pro Tem Scramble. The Bob Moretti Scholarship Foundation is a nonprofit organization established as a charitable corporation under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. It has a board of 15 members, only four of whom are "candidates" or agents, etc. of candidates. Eleven, or two- thirds of the members, are not candidates, their agents, or relatives of the candidates. The Speaker of the Assembly and the President pro Tempore of the Senate have served as co-chairs of the foundation in the past. They are not involved in the day-to-day functioning of the foundation. However, they have traditionally chaired the annual meeting at which scholarship recipients are determined. It has been traditional for the Speaker of the Assembly and the President pro Tem to sign solicitation letters in connection with the event and to lend their names to the annual event. It is contemplated that solicitation letters signed by the Speaker of the Assembly and the President pro Tempore seeking contributions to the Bob Moretti Memorial Scholarship Foundation will be sent to a wide list of potential donors, which includes entities that are lobbyist employers, lobbying firms, and lobbyists.
